question: When using the full deduction method: taxpayer earns 50,000 in residence country and 20,000 abroad (SO 30%). PO rate is 20%:
options: The taxpayer will be paying 14,000 as PO in its residency country
The taxpayer will be paying 10,000 as PO in its residency country
The taxpayer will be generating a negative tax effect for an amount of 2,000 in its residency country
The taxpayer will pay a global tax of 20,000 (PO+SO)
explanation: Full deduction: PO on global income = 70,000 × 20% = 14,000. Deduct full SO tax paid = 20,000 × 30% = 6,000. PO net = 14,000 - 6,000 = 8,000. But local PO on 50,000 only = 10,000. The SO deduction (6,000) exceeds the local PO attributable to foreign income (20,000 × 20% = 4,000), creating a subsidy/negative tax of 4,000 - 6,000 = -2,000. This means the full deduction creates a subsidy effect of 2,000.

question: If a country wants to incentivize its resident taxpayers (under progressive tax) to do business abroad, a valid option would be:
options: Using the limited deduction method to avoid double taxation instead of full exemption method
Fully exempt taxpayers on residency country income
Not signing any double taxation convention
Create some additional taxation on Virtual Permanent Establishments located in the country
explanation: Wait — this seems counterintuitive. Full exemption of foreign income (and also domestic income?) seems extreme. More logically: offering full exemption on foreign income (not all income) would incentivize taxpayers to invest abroad. None of the options is perfect, but the exam answer is b. Exempting foreign income makes foreign investment more attractive from a tax perspective.

question: The main difference between Internal CUP and External CUP is:
options: Internal CUP can only be applied on almost identical products, while external CUP is used on products that are non-easily comparable
Internal CUP can only be used on transactions performed in the same country, while external CUP is used in international business
External CUP will be used by companies that are exclusively selling to group companies products that can be easily compared to other products sold in market conditions
External CUP is used when Cost Plus or Resale Methods are not applicable due to market restrictions such as anti-dumping laws
explanation: Internal CUP: the tested company itself sells the same (or similar) product to both group and third-party customers — so you can compare the group price with the third-party price directly. External CUP: no internal comparable exists, so you use prices between two independent third parties in the market. Both require high product comparability.

question: When an American multinational creates a Dutch holding company that owns EU subsidiaries:
options: This Dutch company will always get both treaty benefits and EU Directive benefits on dividends
This Dutch company will always be treated as a letterbox company and banned from DTC advantages
Will be fully entitled to claim a tax sparing clause
The acceptancy of the holding company could be challenged by the real beneficial owner concept under the PPT
explanation: The Principal Purpose Test (BEPS Action 6, Article 29 OECD Model) allows tax authorities to deny treaty benefits if obtaining those benefits was one of the principal purposes of the arrangement. An interposed Dutch holding without genuine substance may fail the PPT or the beneficial ownership test.

question: When an entity is deemed to have a PE in another country:
options: The PE profit would be taxed in the other country according to the SO principle
The PE profit would be taxed in the other country according to the PO principle
The PE profit would be tax exempted in the other country
There will never be a taxable profit in the PE
explanation: PE profits are taxed in the country where the PE is located under the Source Obligation principle. The PE does not have PO (it is not a resident taxpayer). The parent company's residence country should exempt or provide a credit for the PE profits to avoid double taxation.

question: The concept 'Harmful tax practice' is:
options: A strategy setup to optimize, in a fully legal way, multinational entities' tax burden
Is something felt by a taxpayer when being considered as tax resident in two countries at the same time
Is produced when two countries sign a DTC which does not follow OECD recommendations
Is one of the BEPS project actions
explanation: Harmful Tax Practices is the subject of BEPS Action 5. It focuses on preferential regimes that attract profit without corresponding economic activity, and on improving transparency through exchange of tax rulings. It is not about legal tax optimization or dual residency.

question: OECD Guidelines:
options: Are fully enforceable for countries given that they guarantee the taxpayer's rights
Qualify as 'soft law' because they are neither strictly binding in nature nor completely lacking legal significance
Are enforceable provisions for the taxpayers and they can even determine penalties in case of non-fulfillment
Should not be given any value as they do not have any legal significance
explanation: OECD Guidelines are soft law — authoritative and widely followed but not legally binding in the strict sense. They are incorporated into many domestic laws and relied upon by courts globally, giving them practical (if not formal) legal significance.

question: According to the OECD DTC Model, a Company's Director remuneration shall be taxed:
options: Only under PO, in the country where the company is tax resident
Only under SO, in the country where the company is tax resident
Under PO, in the country where the company is tax resident and under SO in the country where the Director is tax resident
Under SO, in the country where the company is tax resident and under PO in the country where the Director is tax resident
explanation: Article 16 OECD Model (Directors' fees): remuneration paid to a director may be taxed in the country where the company is resident (SO — source is the payer's country). The director is taxed under PO in the country of their own residence. So: company country = SO; director's country = PO.

question: Individual in country A earns 100,000 and 50,000 in country B (SO 10%). Country A progressive brackets (cumulative): 0–40K: 10%; 40,001–80K: 20%; 80,001–120K: 30%; above 120K: 40%. Country A uses exemption with progressivity on taxed foreign income:
options: PO tax will be 34,000 euros
PO tax will be 18,000 euros
SO tax will be 12,000 euros
PO+SO tax will be 34,000 euros
explanation: SO tax = 50,000 × 10% = 5,000. For PO: global income = 150,000. Tax on 150,000: 40K×10% + 40K×20% + 40K×30% + 30K×40% = 4,000+8,000+12,000+12,000 = 36,000. Average rate on 150K = 36,000/150,000 = 24%. Apply 24% to domestic income (100,000): 24,000. But wait — PO tax on 100K at the rate triggered by 150K. Actually: the rate applicable = 36,000/150,000 = 24%. PO = 100,000 × 24% = 24,000. Hmm, doesn't match any option cleanly. Alternative: effective rate on 100K only = (4,000+8,000+12,000)/100,000 = 24,000/100,000 = 24%. PO = 24,000? Total = 24,000 + 5,000 = 29,000. Option d = 34,000. This is the 2024 = 2025 repeat question — answer per official exam marking is d.

question: From a tax point of view, the main difference between a Subsidiary and a Permanent Establishment in another country is:
options: The subsidiary structure will suffer from the underlying tax effect while the PE structure will only suffer from the legal double taxation effect
The PE structure will suffer from the underlying tax effect while the subsidiary structure will only suffer from the legal double taxation effect
None, both structures will be taxed under similar principles
Both the subsidiary structure and the PE structure will suffer from the legal double taxation effect
explanation: Subsidiary: separate legal entity → distributes dividends → triggers underlying tax (economic double taxation: profits taxed at subsidiary level + at parent level). PE: legally part of the parent → profit repatriation is not a dividend → only legal double taxation risk (same profits taxed in source country and at the parent's residence).

question: The 'Sinatra' clause used in the OECD DTC model:
options: Has widely superseded by the 'Taylor Swift' clause in the MLI, to avoid profit relocation
Sets some caveats in the OECD DTC model to prevent over-indebtment between Multinational Companies
Does not allow artists' income being artificially tagged as 'business profits' to benefit from lower tax rates or exemptions in the SO country
Is a recommendation of the OECD to tax artists only in the PO country
explanation: The 'Sinatra clause' (Article 17 OECD Model — Entertainers and Sportspersons) ensures that income earned by artists and sportspeople from activities performed in a country can be taxed in that source country — preventing artists from labelling performance income as 'business profits' to escape source taxation.

question: Setting non-market prices between two related entities in countries with a DTC may lead into:
options: A double taxation issue if two different tax authorities challenge prices and obtain different results
The application of the profit split method by the affected tax authorities
Is fully allowed in the case that both entities are having tax losses
May generate a custom duty underpayment
explanation: If both tax authorities independently adjust the same intragroup price and reach different arm's length results, the same profit may end up being taxed twice. The MAP (Mutual Agreement Procedure) and bilateral APAs exist to prevent this — but they require both authorities to agree.

question: According to the EU 'Parent and Subsidiary' Directive:
options: Interest payments made between associated enterprises should not be taxed under SO to avoid discrimination
In general, all dividend payments made between all entities within EU countries are not taxed under SO
In general, all dividend payments made between group companies in EU countries are not taxed under SO
Parent and subsidiary structures will suffer from the underlying tax effect, but may claim a tax refund at the residency country of the Parent Co.
explanation: The Parent-Subsidiary Directive (PSD) eliminates withholding tax on dividends distributed from an EU subsidiary to a qualifying EU parent (generally ≥10% shareholding held for ≥1 year). It covers group companies — not all entities. Option b (all entities) is too broad. Option a describes the Interest & Royalties Directive.

question: When a private person, acting as final consumer, imports goods into their EU country:
options: May have to pay VAT, custom duties and externality taxes at the border, depending on the nature of the goods imported
May have to pay GST, custom duties and externality taxes at the border, depending on the nature of the goods imported
May have to pay VAT and custom duties at the border, depending on the nature of the goods imported
May have to pay only VAT depending on the nature of the goods imported
explanation: When importing into the EU as a final consumer, three types of charges may apply: (1) VAT (charged on all goods entering free circulation), (2) customs duties (charged on goods from outside the EU, depending on origin and nature), and (3) externality taxes/excise duties (e.g. on alcohol, tobacco, fuel). All three can be triggered simultaneously.

question: Following OECD guidelines regarding Transfer Pricing rules, it is recommended to:
options: Always use cost plus as method to analyze if the market price rule has been followed
Use different approaches depending on the nature of the intragroup operations to be analyzed
Use the real beneficial owner principle when performing a benchmark
Detail in the Master File the process followed to check if market price rule has been followed
explanation: The OECD recommends a 'most appropriate method' approach: the TP method should be selected based on the nature of the transaction, the availability of comparables, and the functional profile of the parties. No single method (like cost plus) is universally prescribed. The Local File (not Master File) details the methodology per transaction.

question: If a country taxes its residents under a flat tax rate only on their local income (ignoring foreign income):
options: We may say this country applies the PO principle to its resident taxpayers
We may say this country applies the PO principle to non-tax resident taxpayers
Will show the same result as if PO principle was applied with full exemption on foreign income
Will increase foreign investment in the country
explanation: PO (personal obligation) normally means taxing residents on worldwide income. If the country only taxes local income, it is technically applying a territorial/source system to residents. However, the practical result is identical to PO with a full exemption on foreign income — option c correctly identifies this equivalence.

question: The self-charge mechanism used by VAT law will be applied:
options: When a business located in an EU country imports goods from abroad
When a business located in an EU country buys goods from other EU countries
Will increase the cost of purchase for this business, regardless of the entity activity
Will generate a tax credit and a tax refund for the entity
explanation: The reverse charge (self-assessment) mechanism applies when an EU business imports goods from outside the EU or receives certain services from abroad. The buyer self-assesses VAT (charges VAT to itself) and simultaneously claims it as input VAT — net cash flow effect is zero for a fully taxable business, but it does not increase cost.

question: The withholding tax system is normally set by countries:
options: Swapping the tax obligation under SO to the payor of the income to the non-tax resident
Is only applied when a Holding company earns dividends in a foreign country and has to pay taxes there
Is regulated on the OECD DTC Model
To avoid lack of reporting obligations under DAC6
explanation: Withholding tax is a mechanism where the payer (e.g. a company paying dividends, interest, or royalties to a non-resident) is made responsible for deducting and remitting tax on behalf of the non-resident recipient. This ensures effective SO tax collection without needing the non-resident to file a return in the source country.

question: Dual tax residency:
options: May happen but the OECD DTC Model will provide the rules to solve the issue
May never happen if there is a Double Taxation Treaty in force
May happen only for individuals but never to entities
May happen when importing goods from a tax haven
explanation: Dual tax residency can happen to both individuals and companies. When a DTC exists, the tie-breaker rules in Article 4 resolve which country is the sole treaty residence country. Without a DTC, dual residency leads to double PO taxation.

question: Today (exam day June 2025):
options: We are all tax residents in Spain for the 2025 calendar year
We are tax residents in Spain for the 2024 calendar year if we were in 2024
It will depend on our nationality
We can't say for sure yet
explanation: On exam day (June 2025), the 2025 tax year is not yet complete — the 183-day threshold has not been crossed for everyone, and year-end has not arrived. Residency for 2025 cannot be confirmed yet. This is a consistent annual exam question.

question: Economic double taxation happens when:
options: Market price rule is not duly applied and tax authorities adjust prices on such transactions
One single person is taxed twice over the same income
Two different persons are taxed over the same income
Can be also referred as 'the underlying tax effect'
explanation: Economic double taxation: same income taxed in the hands of two different persons (e.g. subsidiary pays corporate tax, then shareholder pays income tax on the same distributed profit). This is also described as the 'underlying tax effect' — both c and d correctly characterise it.

question: If a tax auditor wants a full picture of the entities forming part of a multinational group — their activities, locations, etc. — this information could be found in:
options: The Local File of the audited entity
The group Master File
The Country by Country Report
The Benchmark
explanation: The Master File provides a high-level overview of the MNE group: organisational structure, description of business activities, intangibles, intercompany financing, and financial positions. This is precisely what an auditor needs to understand the group as a whole — not just the specific entity being audited.

question: Under a progressive tax rate: 0–200,000: 10%; 200,001–500,000: 20%; +500,000: 30%. Individual earns 400,000:
options: The average rate is 20%
The marginal tax rate over each new 100,000 earned would be 20%
The marginal tax rate is 15%
All income would be taxed at 20% tax rate
explanation: Tax = 200,000 × 10% + 200,000 × 20% = 20,000 + 40,000 = 60,000. Average rate = 60,000/400,000 = 15%. Marginal rate = 20% (income falls in 200,001–500,000 bracket). Each additional 100,000 would also be taxed at 20% until the 500,000 threshold. Option b is correct.

question: Action 4 under BEPS focuses on interest deduction between associated companies:
options: To avoid profit shifting by means of using too high interest rates on loans between associated entities
To avoid profit shifting by means of using non-economic debt/net worth ratios, generating over-indebtedness in group entities
To avoid the misuse of the TNMM as method to calculate the market price interest rate
To avoid the use of hybrid mismatches as a tax evasion strategy
explanation: BEPS Action 4 targets excessive intragroup debt that artificially inflates interest deductions — not the interest rate itself (that's a TP issue handled by Action 8-10). The concern is the volume of debt creating high debt/EBITDA ratios that would not exist between independent parties. Hybrid mismatches are Action 2.

question: Creating an entity in a country because it has low tax rates and many DTCs signed with other countries:
options: Could be forbidden by tax authorities of affected countries and treated as tax evasion
Will always be valid and recommendable following OECD guidelines
Could be challenged and tax benefits rejected if the PPT doesn't show real business activity
Is forbidden according to Pillar 2 and DAC6 guidelines
explanation: Under BEPS Action 6, the Principal Purpose Test (PPT) denies treaty benefits if obtaining those benefits was one of the principal purposes of the arrangement. Without genuine economic substance, a conduit entity may have treaty benefits denied. It is not automatically illegal or always valid — the PPT test is applied on the facts.

question: According to the OECD DTC Model, when dealing with immovable property income:
options: Each country has the right to set its own definition of immovable property that may not match with the other country
The immovable property definition is outlined in the DTC and will be the one applicable by the signing countries
It includes the profit generated when the immovable property is sold
Fictional income arising from self-use of such properties is not included in this income category
explanation: Article 6(2) OECD Model defines immovable property — the DTC definition prevails for treaty purposes. The DTC definition typically refers to domestic law of the source country for specifics, but the treaty itself provides a framework definition. Capital gains on immovable property are covered by Article 13. Self-use income can be included if domestic law deems it.

question: The PO and SO taxation principles:
options: Will never generate a double taxation issue from a single country point of view as they target different taxpayers
Will never generate an international double taxation issue when two countries are involved
Could generate double taxation issues that will be always solved by the SO country thanks to the exemption method
PO and SO will always generate double taxation issues
explanation: From a single country's perspective, PO (applied to residents) and SO (applied to non-residents) target different taxpayer categories — so no single taxpayer suffers double taxation domestically from these two principles alone. International double taxation arises when multiple countries apply their own PO and SO rules to the same cross-border income.

question: Under Transfer Pricing regulations, the resale minus method to determine if intragroup transactions are at market price:
options: Uses the purchase cost to third parties of the analyzed entity, and applies a discount to this cost to find the market price sale price
Forms part of the Transactional Net Margin Method analysis to validate conclusions obtained after the benchmark
Needs to be modulated by a functional and risk analysis
Does not exist — the correct name is resale plus method
explanation: The Resale Minus (Resale Price Method): starts from the resale price to third parties and subtracts a gross margin to arrive at the arm's length purchase price. Any TP method needs to be informed by the functional and risk analysis to ensure the selected margin is appropriate for the functions performed. Options a and d have factual errors.

question: When a PE is deemed to exist in country A and generates profits selling in country A and other countries:
options: Income obtained by the PE will be taxed according to the Source Obligation principle in country A
Income obtained by the PE will be taxed according to the Personal Obligation principle in country A
Income obtained by the PE will never be taxed in country A if there is a DTC that follows OECD model
Income obtained by the PE will be taxed if the PE lasts more than 12 months in country A
explanation: Once a PE exists in country A, country A taxes the profits attributable to that PE under SO. Only the profits attributable to the PE (Article 7) are taxed — not the entire company's global profits. The PE's taxation in country A is a source-based obligation, regardless of duration (PE duration threshold determines whether the PE exists; once it does, attribution rules apply).

question: According to the OECD DTC, business profits not obtained through a PE should only be taxed at the residency country (PO). There is a principle of prevalence where:
options: SO federal or regional taxes will always be applicable
If the treaty allows SO on some income detailed separately, this last rule will be considered as a 'Tax Sparing' clause
This will help avoiding the double taxation effect
If the treaty allows SO on some income detailed separately, this last rule may prevail over the business profit rule
explanation: Article 7 establishes the general rule (residence taxation for business profits without PE). However, specific income articles (e.g. Article 10 dividends, 11 interest, 12 royalties) may grant source taxation rights — those specific rules prevail over the general business profits rule. This is the lex specialis principle within the DTC.

question: Mary is tax resident in country 1. Jan–Sep: worked for company Z (country 1), earning 50,000. Oct–Dec: worked in country 2 as salesperson for company X (country 1), earning 15,000. Both countries have a DTC following OECD model:
options: Her taxable base under PO will be 65,000 in country 1 and 15,000 under SO in country 2
Her taxable base under PO will be 65,000 in country 1 and no SO will be levied in country 2
Her taxable base under PO will be 50,000 in country 1 and 15,000 under SO in country 2
Her taxable base under PO will be 15,000 in country 1 and 50,000 under SO in country 2
explanation: Article 15 OECD Model (Employment income): employment income may be taxed at source only if the employee is present in country 2 for more than 183 days in 12 months, AND/OR the employer is resident or has a PE in country 2. Mary's employer (company X) is based in country 1 — so the 183-day test and employer residency test both favour country 1. No SO in country 2. Full 65,000 is taxed under PO in country 1 (with potential relief for foreign income).

question: Under ATAD3:
options: We are dealing with an OECD guideline to avoid tax evasion
It is an EU Directive focusing on the 'substance' principle for entities established in its territory
It is a reporting obligation where entities or their lawyers and tax advisors have to inform about restructuring at international level
Focus on making entities pay taxes where they are really generating their profits
explanation: ATAD3 (the 'UNSHELL' Directive proposal) targets shell entities lacking minimum economic substance in EU member states. Entities failing the substance criteria are denied DTC and EU Directive benefits. It is an EU legislative initiative (Directive), not an OECD guideline. ATAD1/ATAD2 cover other anti-avoidance measures.

question: A company buys wood chairs locally (100,000 input VAT) and re-sells them to businesses outside the country (no output VAT charged):
options: The company will be entitled to apply for a full input VAT refund
The company shall treat the paid input VAT as a cost because it is not generating any output VAT
The company will have to amend its invoices to foreign businesses and apply output VAT
The company shall claim its suppliers to amend their invoices and reissue them without input VAT
explanation: Sales to businesses outside the country (exports or intra-EU B2B supplies) are zero-rated — the seller charges 0% but retains the right to recover all input VAT. This is a fundamental VAT principle: exporters should not bear VAT costs that would make their goods uncompetitive internationally. A full VAT refund is available.

question: The main difference between Internal CUP and External CUP is:
options: Internal CUP can be applied on almost identical products sold both to third parties and group entities, while external CUP is used on products that are non-easily comparable
Internal CUP can only be used on transactions performed in the same country, while external CUP is used in international business
Internal CUP will be used by companies that are exclusively selling to group companies products that could not be easily compared to other products sold in market conditions
External CUP is to be used when Cost Plus or Resale Methods are not applicable due to market restrictions, such as anti-dumping laws
explanation: Internal CUP: the company itself transacts the same (or comparable) product with both related and unrelated parties — the internal third-party transaction is the comparable. External CUP: no internal comparable exists; you find comparables between two independent parties in the market. Both are high-quality methods when applicable.

question: Comparing full exemption vs limited tax credit: PO rate 20%, SO rate 30%, taxpayer only has 1,000 income in the SO country:
options: Both methods correct the double taxation issue and global taxes paid are 200 in both cases
Exemption method helps correct the DT issue as global taxation will be 200, compared to the exemption method that gives 300
Both methods correct the double taxation issue and global taxes paid are 300 in both cases
The PO country is granting a subsidy to the taxpayer under the limited tax credit method
explanation: Full exemption: PO taxes 0 (exempt), SO = 1,000 × 30% = 300. Global = 300. Limited tax credit: PO on 1,000 = 200, credit limited to PO rate (20%) = 200. SO = 300. Net PO = 200 - 200 = 0. Global = 300. So both give 300 total? But wait: under full exemption, PO country exempts → PO = 0. Total = 300 (SO only). Under limited credit: credit capped at 200, PO = 0, SO = 300. Total = 300. Both = 300. But option a says 200... The answer per exam is a, which seems to imply the global tax is 200 — this may assume SO = PO rate which contradicts SO = 30%. The intended exam answer is a.

question: Why is exemption with progressivity fairer than full exemption in a country with progressive taxes?
options: It helps generating progressivity as the average tax rate is increased but does not tax already taxed foreign income
A progressive tax system needs to increase the average tax rate as much as possible to make rich pay more on both local and foreign income
Exemption with progressivity is unfair and inequal in a country with progressive tax rates
Exemption with progressivity does not tax foreign income nor takes it into account to tax the rest of income
explanation: Exemption with progressivity maintains tax fairness: foreign income is not taxed domestically (respecting the DTA obligation), but it is taken into account to determine the rate applied to domestic income. This preserves the progressive rate structure — wealthier taxpayers with foreign income still face higher average rates on their domestic income, unlike under full exemption which ignores foreign income entirely.

question: What's a MAP (Mutual Agreement Procedure)?
options: A procedure found in the OECD DTC Model providing a compelling obligation of signatory countries to solve any controversy arising from the treaty in a friendly manner
An action found in the ATAD3 regulations
The so-called Multilateral Agreement on Prices helping solve issues on Transfer Pricing regulations
The so-called 'More Are Prosecuted act' trying to send tax evaders to jail
explanation: The MAP (Article 25 OECD Model) is a dispute resolution mechanism allowing taxpayers to request competent authorities to resolve cases of double taxation or taxation not in accordance with the treaty. It involves negotiation between tax authorities, not a court. BEPS Action 14 improved its effectiveness.

question: When a private person is thinking about investing in another country, SO taxes, DTC existence, and relief methods at their PO country:
options: Are the key drivers to decide to invest or not in another country
Should be variables in a more complex decision that involves return on investment, currency risk, political stability, etc.
Are not an issue if SO > PO and limited deduction is the way double taxation is relieved
Taxation is not a variable to consider when investing abroad
explanation: While tax is an important consideration, investment decisions involve multiple factors: expected return on investment, political and regulatory risk, currency risk, market size, exit options, legal system, and yes — tax treatment. Taxation alone should not drive investment decisions.

question: According to the OECD DTC Model, when a person is selling shares of an entity located in the other country:
options: This income qualifies as capital gain and following OECD recommendations should only be taxed in the PO country
This income qualifies as a dividend and following OECD recommendations should only be taxed in the PO country, allowing SO at 15% max if 50%+ participation
This income qualifies as capital gain and should only be taxed in PO country unless the entity owns real estate, even if a small part of assets
This income qualifies as capital gain and following OECD recommendations should only be taxed in PO country unless the entity owns real estate weighing more than 50% of its assets
explanation: Article 13 OECD Model: capital gains on shares are generally taxed only at the residence (PO) country. Exception: if the entity's assets consist principally of (>50%) immovable property located in the other country, the source country may also tax the gain. This prevents circumventing Article 6 by holding real estate through a company.

question: An AI tax advisory platform in Country A serves clients in Country B (monthly subscription). Which statement best reflects current OECD approach?
options: The income may be taxed in Country B if its domestic law recognizes a digital PE or significant economic presence, even without physical presence
The income should be taxed exclusively in Country A as the place of management is there and no PE exists in Country B
The income should be taxed in Country B as the platform's digital interface constitutes a fixed place of business under Article 5
The income qualifies as capital gain under Article 12 and is subject to withholding tax in Country B
explanation: The OECD (Pillar 1, digital economy) acknowledges that traditional PE rules based on physical presence are insufficient for the digital economy. Some countries have unilaterally introduced digital PE or significant economic presence concepts. The current OECD approach is working toward a multilateral solution (Pillar 1 Amount A), but interim measures include DSTs. Option a correctly reflects the current state of play.

question: When a country aims to avoid being classified as a 'non-cooperative jurisdiction' by the OECD, it must:
options: Ensure high personal income tax rates
Allow banking secrecy laws to remain intact
Commit to exchange of information and implement transparency standards
Avoid signing any double tax treaties
explanation: The OECD/G20 Global Forum on Transparency and Exchange of Information evaluates jurisdictions on tax transparency and information exchange. To avoid blacklisting, a jurisdiction must commit to automatic exchange of financial account information (CRS), exchange tax rulings, and implement the CbCR framework.

question: Which DTC provision is most likely to be challenged under anti-avoidance rules if used to shift investments for tax minimization?
options: Exemption on source income
Treaty shopping through an intermediary country
Entry into force clause
Mutual Agreement Procedure
explanation: Treaty shopping (using an intermediary country's DTC to access benefits not otherwise available) is the paradigmatic anti-avoidance target. BEPS Action 6 (PPT and LOB) is specifically designed to prevent it. The entry into force clause and MAP are procedural provisions; source income exemptions are legitimate DTC benefits.

question: Individual in country A earns 100,000 and 50,000 in country B (SO 10%). Country A brackets (cumulative): 0–40K: 10%; 40,001–80K: 20%; 80,001–120K: 30%; above 120K: 40%. Country A uses exemption with progressivity:
options: PO tax will be 34,000 euros
PO tax will be 18,000 euros
SO tax will be 12,000 euros
PO+SO tax will be 34,000 euros
explanation: SO = 50,000 × 10% = 5,000. Global income = 150,000. Tax on 150,000: 40K×10% + 40K×20% + 40K×30% + 30K×40% = 4,000+8,000+12,000+12,000 = 36,000. Average rate = 36,000/150,000 = 24%. Apply 24% rate to domestic income (100,000): PO = 24,000. Total PO+SO = 24,000+5,000 = 29,000. This doesn't match d (34,000). If we apply the rate to global income attributable: alternative calculation applies each bracket to 100,000 using the blended rate. The official exam answer is d = 34,000. One possible route: PO = 40K×10%+40K×20%+20K×30% = 4K+8K+6K = 18,000... but 18K = option b. The question appears in both 2024 and 2025 exams. Verify against official solution.

question: A Portuguese company plans to fund a Polish subsidiary (€12M) with €400K equity and €11.6M shareholder loan. From an international tax planning perspective:
options: This strategy is not acceptable due to OECD BEPS Action 4, which prohibits interest-bearing loans between parent companies and subsidiaries
The financing method is tax-efficient as Poland allows unlimited interest deductions on loans from foreign parent companies
This method is fully valid as long as the interest rate applied is aligned with arm's length standards
While the strategy is feasible, Polish transfer pricing and thin capitalization rules must be considered to understand the tax consequences for the Group
explanation: BEPS Action 4 does not prohibit intercompany loans — it limits interest deductions to a fixed ratio of EBITDA (typically 10-30%). An arm's length rate is necessary but not sufficient; the volume of debt (97% leverage) may breach thin capitalization rules. The strategy is feasible but requires careful analysis of Polish domestic BEPS implementation.

question: Austrian taxpayer earns €2,000 in Hungary (10% withholding) and €6,000 in Austria. Austria's effective tax rate on total income is 25%. Austria applies exemption with progression. Correct treatment:
options: The Hungarian income will be exempt from taxation in Austria and will not influence the rate applied to Austrian income
The taxpayer will benefit from a full deduction of Hungarian tax, which reduces the final Austrian tax liability
Although exempt, the Hungarian income increases the effective marginal rate on domestic income, leading to higher taxation of the €6,000
Austria taxes the entire €8,000 at 25% and provides a credit for the tax already paid in Hungary
explanation: Under exemption with progressivity: the €2,000 Hungarian income is exempt from Austrian tax, but is included in the tax base to determine the rate applicable to the €6,000 Austrian income. Since the rate on €8,000 (25%) > rate on €6,000 alone, the Austrian income is taxed at a higher rate than it would be without the foreign income. This is the progressivity effect — higher taxation of domestic income without taxing foreign income.

question: Which scenario is most likely to give rise to a PE under a DTC with a service PE clause?
options: A Spanish company provides remote accounting services to a French client for 10 months, without any employee travelling to France
A Dutch firm sends employees to Ireland to supervise a construction project for 5 months under subcontracting
An Australian consultant spends 4 months a year in Singapore advising a local client on a continuous basis for 3 consecutive years
A Brazilian firm appoints an agent in Argentina who, though formally independent, works exclusively for the Brazilian firm and routinely negotiates contracts on its behalf
explanation: Option d: an agent who is formally independent but acts exclusively for one principal and habitually concludes/negotiates contracts constitutes a dependent agent PE (Article 5(5)/(6) OECD Model). The formal independence is disregarded when the agent is de facto economically dependent. Options a (no physical presence) and b (5 months, below 12-month threshold) don't clearly meet PE thresholds. Option c (4 months/year = cumulative issue) may qualify under some service PE clauses, but option d is the clearest case.

question: Under the EU Parent-Subsidiary Directive, dividends from a subsidiary to its parent are exempt from withholding tax when:
options: The parent is in an EU Member State, holds at least 10% of the subsidiary in another Member State, and both are subject to corporate tax
The directive applies only when dividends have already been taxed at the subsidiary level
The parent is fully tax-exempt in its residence state but may still benefit if it holds over 10% of the subsidiary
The parent and subsidiary are both EU-resident, the parent holds 20% of the subsidiary, but only for 4 months
explanation: PSD conditions: (1) parent in one EU Member State, (2) subsidiary in another EU Member State, (3) minimum 10% participation (some member states may apply lower threshold), (4) minimum holding period (typically 2 years, which can be met post-distribution with a commitment), and (5) both entities are subject to corporate income tax. Option c (tax-exempt parent) generally does not qualify. Option d (4 months) fails the holding period requirement.

question: Which statement reflects the objectives and scope of OECD Pillar 1 and Pillar 2?
options: Pillar 1 introduces a 15% minimum global tax rate for all MNE groups with revenues over €750M, while Pillar 2 reallocates taxing rights over residual profits

Pillar 1 reallocates part of taxing rights over profits of certain large MNEs to market jurisdictions, while Pillar 2 introduces a 15% global minimum effective tax rate for MNE groups with revenues exceeding €750M
Pillar 1 and 2 both apply exclusively to digital platform businesses operating in more than three jurisdictions with over €750M in digital revenue
Pillar 1 aims to eliminate double taxation through hybrid mismatch rules, and Pillar 2 requires withholding taxes in low-tax jurisdictions
explanation: Pillar 1: reallocates 'Amount A' (a portion of residual profits of the largest and most profitable MNEs, revenue >€20B and profitability >10%) to market jurisdictions regardless of physical presence. Pillar 2 (GloBE rules): introduces a 15% global minimum effective tax rate for MNE groups with consolidated revenue >€750M, using IIR, UTPR, and QDMTT mechanisms.

question: The sequence PO-SO-PO:
options: Is a famous Mexican dish recipe
Is an example of legal double taxation
Is an example of economic double taxation
Will happen when paying dividends at international scale
explanation: PO-SO-PO: (1) the subsidiary earns profit and pays corporate income tax (PO in subsidiary's country), (2) the source country withholds on the dividend distribution (SO), (3) the parent company is taxed on the dividend received (PO in parent's country). The same profit is taxed in the hands of two different persons — subsidiary then parent — which is the definition of economic double taxation. And it literally is what happens when dividends are paid internationally.

question: BEPS Action 1 on Tax Challenges arising from Digitalization:
options: Has concluded that any profits obtained by a group of companies have to be reported through the Country by Country Report
Has been closed because no agreement has been reached by the international community in this regard
Is focusing on finding a common approach regarding profits and taxing rights allocation as well as defining the mechanisms to ensure the avoidance of BEPS practices
Has been subject to a consultation in front of the European Court of Justice to determine compatibility with the EU Treaties
explanation: BEPS Action 1 on the digital economy focuses on finding a common international approach to allocate profits and taxing rights (Pillar 1) and ensuring minimum taxation (Pillar 2). No agreement was reached initially but work continues under the Inclusive Framework. The ECJ has no role here.

question: Treaty shopping refers to:
options: A legal and accepted possibility to freely choose the best jurisdiction to formally set up a company with no real activity in order to benefit from the DTC network of that country
Is covered under Article 7 of the OECD Model Convention and provides for the better location for a permanent establishment
A negotiation technique used by countries in order to achieve better conditions in their respective bilateral Double Tax Convention
A tax avoidance measure aimed at benefiting from Double Tax Conventions without being entitled to through the use of interposed instrumental entities
explanation: Treaty shopping is the practice of interposing an entity in a third country to access DTC benefits to which the ultimate beneficial owner would not otherwise be entitled. It is a tax avoidance practice targeted by BEPS Action 6 (PPT and LOB rules). It is not legal or accepted under current international standards.

question: In International Taxation 'hybrids':
options: Refer to countries where there are different regimes depending on the region where the person lives
Refer to situations which may lead to double non-taxation as a consequence of a mismatch in the tax treatment of an instrument or an entity in two or more different countries
Are those territories having signed exchange of information agreements with the EU but who are still considered as low tax jurisdictions
Refer to the possibility to deduct at the residence country a higher tax than the one paid at the source country
explanation: Hybrid mismatches exploit differences in how two countries classify an instrument or entity — producing deduction/no-inclusion outcomes (double non-taxation). BEPS Action 2 targets these arrangements through coordinated domestic and treaty-level rules.

question: The three-tier approach in transfer pricing refers to:
options: The standard transfer pricing documentation to be produced by multinational groups
The interquartile range to be considered to determine transfer prices
The number of available databases to carry out a benchmark analysis
The number of traditional transaction valuation methods
explanation: The three-tier TP documentation package under BEPS Action 13 consists of: (1) Master File — group-level overview, (2) Local File — entity-specific transaction analysis, and (3) Country by Country Report — jurisdiction-by-jurisdiction financial data.

question: The OECD BEPS Project:
options: Encourages multinational groups to relocate profits in low taxation countries
Refers to situations leading to double taxation which are therefore usually avoided by taxpayers
Has abolished the arm's length principle because it leads to many problems
Promotes necessary changes in domestic and international tax regulations to allocate taxable profits where the taxpayer's real economic activity is carried out
explanation: BEPS aims to align taxation with where economic activity occurs and value is created. It does not encourage profit shifting — it combats it. The arm's length principle remains the foundation of TP rules under BEPS.

question: For international taxation purposes 'the habitual abode':
options: Has an impact to determine entities' tax residency when there is a residency conflict and a Double Tax Convention is applicable
Corresponds to the country from which an individual is a national
Is one of the criteria provided by the tie-breaker rule contained in article 4 of Double Tax Conventions and generally refers to the place where the person stays longer within a tax period
Is one of the criteria provided by the tie-breaker rule contained in article 4 of Double Tax Conventions and generally refers to the place where the person has a permanent home
explanation: The habitual abode is the third tie-breaker in Article 4(2) OECD Model for individuals — it refers to the country where the person stays most frequently (counted by days of presence). Option d describes the 'permanent home' criterion, which is the first tie-breaker, not habitual abode. The habitual abode applies only to individuals, not entities.

question: Double tax relief methods (exemption or tax credit ones):
options: Apply at the level of the source country to avoid or reduce double taxation
Are only applicable when the taxpayer is a national of the country and has been living there for at least 5 years
Apply at the level of the taxpayer's residence country to avoid or reduce double taxation
Are exclusively provided by Double Tax Conventions but never by domestic law
explanation: Relief methods (exemption and credit/deduction) are applied by the residence (PO) country. They can be provided either by domestic law or by DTCs — not exclusively by DTCs. Nationality and years of residency are irrelevant.

question: Economic double taxation implies:
options: Taxation of the same income obtained in similar economic activities
Taxation of the same income by two similar taxes in the same period on two different persons
Taxation of the same taxable event because of dual residency situation
Taxation of the same taxable event, same period and same or similar tax on the same person
explanation: Economic double taxation: same income taxed in two different persons' hands (e.g. corporate profits taxed at company level, then dividends taxed at shareholder level). Option d describes legal double taxation (same person). Option c describes a dual residency scenario.

question: The OECD Model Tax Convention:
options: Is a guide and a reference for the negotiation and interpretation of bilateral double tax conventions
Has been abolished by the BEPS Project and now the countries will only apply the Multilateral Instrument
Has been transposed to EU countries domestic law by means of Directives and then is fully binding for EU countries
Is very controversial because only serves to western industrialized countries' interests
explanation: The OECD Model is a non-binding reference document — a guide and starting point for DTC negotiations. It has not been abolished by BEPS (the MLI modifies existing treaties to incorporate BEPS recommendations). The EU Directives are separate instruments and do not incorporate the OECD Model directly.

question: The arm's length principle implies that:
options: The tax administrations should assume that associated enterprises manipulate their profits
Associated enterprises cannot engage into transactions with each other unless approved by the corresponding authorities
Associated enterprises must always determine prices in their common transactions through a benchmark analysis
Parity of tax treatment for members of MNE Groups and independent enterprises can be achieved
explanation: The arm's length principle (Article 9 OECD Model) seeks fiscal parity between MNE group transactions and those between independent enterprises. Tax authorities do not automatically assume manipulation. A benchmark is one tool but not always mandatory.

question: We can say that the current international transparency standards:
options: Have improved tax compliance and help to prevent base erosion and profit shifting
Are exclusively based on exchange of information upon request because most countries have opposed to the automatic standard
Have implied the growth of bank secrecy practices
Have a reduced scope and only apply in connection with transfer pricing issues
explanation: The current standard — driven by BEPS Action 5 and the OECD's Common Reporting Standard (CRS) — is automatic exchange of information. This has significantly improved transparency and tax compliance. Bank secrecy has been reduced, not grown.

question: By underlying tax we mean:
options: A tax applied at the lowest marginal rate
Withholding tax paid at the source country on a dividend distribution
Tax paid by a company in a low tax jurisdiction
Corporate Income Tax paid by a subsidiary on the profits obtained which are further distributed as dividends
explanation: The underlying tax is the corporate income tax already paid by the subsidiary on the profits from which dividends are later distributed. It creates economic double taxation. The withholding tax on the distribution is a separate, additional layer.

question: If an entity is taxed on royalties received in both the residence and the source country, this leads to:
options: Economic double taxation
A sound base to file a claim before the Human Rights Court
Legal double taxation
A transfer pricing issue
explanation: The same person (the royalty recipient) is taxed on the same income in two countries — that is the definition of legal double taxation. Economic double taxation would involve two different persons being taxed on the same underlying income.

question: According to article 5 of the OECD Model DTC, a permanent establishment will be deemed to exist when:
options: A company has a building site in another country regardless the duration of the construction or installation works
A company has in another country a stock of goods to carry out storage, display or delivery activities
A company carries out its activities in another country through a fixed place of business such as a branch, an office or a place of extraction of natural resources, among other situations
A company has in another country an independent agent acting on its behalf
explanation: Article 5(1) OECD Model: a PE is a fixed place of business through which the business is wholly or partly carried on — including branches, offices, factories, workshops, mines. Building sites require a minimum duration (12 months). Storage/display is specifically excluded. Independent agents (acting in ordinary course of their business) do not create a PE.

question: A DTC provides that tax on interest may be levied by the source country at a maximum rate of 10%. If source country domestic law provides for a 5% tax rate on interest, then:
options: Tax to be paid at source will be 10%
Tax to be paid at source will be 10% but the residence country will grant a tax credit for 5%
No tax will be paid because there is a contradiction between the DTC and domestic law
Tax to be paid at source will be 5%
explanation: The DTC sets a ceiling (maximum 10%). If domestic law is more favourable (5%), the taxpayer benefits from the lower domestic rate. There is no contradiction — the DTC limits the source country's right to tax but cannot force it to tax higher than its domestic law.

question: A Mutual Agreement Procedure:
options: Can only be requested and accepted in case of transfer pricing adjustments
Is a procedure of discussion between the tax authorities of two or more countries and cannot be closed until a decision is reached
Has been submitted to a thorough review under the BEPS Project for efficiency sake in order to enlarge its scope and effectiveness
Will be suppressed and substituted by an Arbitration procedure
explanation: BEPS Action 14 (Making Dispute Resolution Mechanisms More Effective) reviewed and strengthened the MAP process — widening its scope and adding minimum standards for timely resolution. The MAP is not limited to TP cases and has not been abolished; arbitration is an addition, not a replacement.

question: When a Double Tax Convention provides for a tax sparing clause:
options: The most efficient scenario for the taxpayer is the application of the exemption method by the residence country
The taxpayer is granted a tax allowance at the source country through a reduction of the applicable tax rate
The taxpayer can only apply the limited credit method at the residence country and thus deduct a lower tax than the one effectively paid
The taxpayer will have a tax benefit at the residence country consisting on the deduction of a higher tax than the one effectively paid at the source country
explanation: A tax sparing clause allows the taxpayer to credit at the residence country a fictitious (or notional) tax — i.e. the tax that would have been payable at source had no incentive been granted. This means crediting more than was actually paid, preserving the value of the source-country incentive for the investor.

question: When referring to an internal comparable, we are considering:
options: A transaction carried out by one of the entities of a multinational group with third non-related parties which can be taken as a reference to determine market value
A comparable which has been obtained through a benchmark analysis which only can be used in reference to domestic transactions
A comparable only known by the tax authorities because of the information they have access to
A transaction carried out between entities of the same multinational group
explanation: An internal comparable (Internal CUP) is a transaction that the tested party itself carries out with independent third parties under comparable conditions. This is distinguished from an external comparable, which is a transaction between two independent third parties found through databases.

question: According to OECD Transfer Pricing guidelines, the Cost Plus method will be a valid option to set up transfer prices:
options: Because under the Cost Plus method we deduct from the sale price the margin applied by comparable entities under market conditions
When goods sold between associated companies are exactly the same that are being sold by third parties under market conditions
When goods sold between associated companies cannot be easily compared to the ones sold under market conditions, but the activity sector and business held can be considered to be the same or similar
Only when we deal with financial transactions because it is not applicable in transactions relating to purchase and sale of goods or services
explanation: Cost Plus is appropriate when products cannot be directly compared to third-party transactions (otherwise CUP would be preferred) but the functional profile of the supplier is comparable to independent parties in the same sector. Option a describes the Resale Minus method. Option b describes a CUP situation.

question: The exemption with progression method implies:
options: The taxpayer will be able to deduct at the residence country taxes paid abroad as long as they have been determined through the application of a progressive scale
The taxpayer will be taxable at the residence country only on income therein obtained but the tax rate will be calculated considering worldwide income
The taxpayer will be taxable at the residence country on worldwide income
The taxpayer may choose between being taxed at the residence country at a flat rate or through a progressive scale with lower tax rates
explanation: Exemption with progressivity: foreign income is exempt from domestic tax (not included in the taxable base) but is taken into account to determine the tax rate applicable to domestic income. The result is a higher average rate on domestic income compared to pure exemption, preserving the progressive structure.

question: The DTC between France and Spain provides that tax on interest may be levied by the source country at a maximum rate of 10%. Source country domestic law provides for 5% withholding. If the Interest and Royalties Directive applies:
options: Tax to be paid at source will be 10%
Tax to be paid at source will be 10% but the residence country will grant a tax credit for 5%
Tax to be paid at source will be 5%
Tax to be paid at source will be 0%
explanation: The EU Interest and Royalties Directive eliminates withholding tax on interest and royalty payments between associated companies in different EU Member States. When the Directive applies, the source country cannot levy any withholding tax — the rate is 0%, regardless of what the DTC or domestic law provides.

question: A Mutual Agreement Procedure:
options: Is the analysis to be carried out by multinational groups in order to find a set of comparable entities to assess that the transfer prices applied respect the arm's length principle
Will not be available in the future given that according to BEPS Action 14 has proven useless in order to solve international tax disputes
Is a dispute resolution mechanism which allows competent tax authorities to engage into bilateral or multilateral negotiations in order to solve a double taxation issue
Can only be used within the European Union under the scope of the EU Arbitration Convention
explanation: The MAP (Article 25 OECD Model) is a dispute resolution mechanism between competent authorities of contracting states to resolve cases of double taxation or incorrect application of the treaty. BEPS Action 14 strengthened, not eliminated, the MAP. It is available globally, not only in the EU.

question: For Covered Tax Agreements, we refer to:
options: Double Tax Conventions which will be updated by the MLI further to the choices and options made by the signatory countries (MLI position)
Double Tax Conventions which have been approved by the members of the Inclusive Framework as fully adapted to the BEPS recommendations
Those Tax Conventions which apart from preventing double taxation provide for full coverage regarding indirect taxes
The only DTCs which after being updated by the MLI will remain into force as those not being covered by the MLI will be automatically terminated
explanation: Covered Tax Agreements (CTAs) are the existing bilateral DTCs that signatory countries have identified as subject to modification by the MLI. Each country makes specific choices (reservations and options) about which provisions of the MLI apply to each of their CTAs.

question: A Spanish company envisages to import electronic devices from China. Is there any advantage in placing goods in a tax free zone when arriving in Spanish territory?
options: The main advantage is that Custom Duties will not be paid even though VAT cannot be avoided
Tax free zones allow the suspension of VAT payment but not of Custom Duties
If the goods are stored in a tax free zone, Custom Duties and VAT can be fully avoided on a permanent basis as tax free zones qualify as tax havens
Tax free zones allow storing goods on a temporary basis with no payment of both Custom Duties and VAT
explanation: Tax free zones (bonded warehouses) suspend both customs duties and VAT while goods are stored. Both become payable only when goods enter free circulation in the EU. This allows deferral and is useful for goods that may be re-exported without entering the EU market.

question: Under BEPS Action 6 on Prevention of tax treaty abuse:
options: It is considered as a legal and accepted practice to freely choose the best jurisdiction to formally set up a company even though this has no real economic activity in order to benefit from the DTC network of that country
Members of the BEPS Inclusive Framework commit to include in their Tax Conventions provisions dealing with treaty shopping
Members of the BEPS Inclusive Framework commit to include in their Tax Conventions anti-fragmentation rules to avoid abuse of the permanent establishment status
New negotiation rules and techniques are provided to countries in order to achieve better conditions in their respective bilateral Double Tax Conventions
explanation: BEPS Action 6 requires Inclusive Framework members to include a minimum standard in their DTCs to prevent treaty shopping — either through the Principal Purpose Test (PPT), a Limitation on Benefits (LOB) clause, or a combination of both. Anti-fragmentation rules are covered by BEPS Action 7 (PE abuse).

question: A German company hires employees in Italy but has no office there; it uses a logistics operator to stock goods for delivery. Under a DTC, the German company can conclude that:
options: Profits obtained in Italy will be only taxable in Germany because no office will be opened in Italian territory
Double taxation may arise depending on how the German company's activities in Italy are structured
Profits obtained in Italy may be taxable in both countries because the German company will be deemed to have a PE in Italy as a consequence of having a stock of goods in that country
Profits obtained in Italy will be only taxable in Italy in accordance with the rules provided by article 7 of the DTC
explanation: The situation is complex: employees working in Italy could create a PE (service PE or dependent agent PE), but the stock of goods for delivery is an explicitly excluded activity under Article 5(4) OECD Model. Whether a PE exists depends on how the activities are structured — hence double taxation may arise depending on the specific facts.

question: The Transactional Net Margin Method (TNMM):
options: Has been rejected as a valid valuation method in accordance with the BEPS recommendations
Is one of the traditional Transaction Methods and as such is of preferential application
Could be a useful method as one of its strengths is that it may be more tolerant to some functional differences than those methods based on gross profit indicators
Is one of the Transactional Profit Methods and implies a division of the net profits obtained in a common transaction
explanation: The TNMM is one of the two Transactional Profit Methods (along with Profit Split). Its key strength is that net profit indicators are less sensitive to functional and product differences than gross margins, making it more widely applicable. Option d describes the Profit Split method.

question: A French company in a multinational group earns a 10% gross margin selling to third parties; a comparable French independent retailer earns 25%. The 10% margin:
options: May be adjusted by the tax authorities as long as the two transactions can be considered as comparable
Will be adjusted by the tax authorities because it is lower than the margin obtained by independent parties
Cannot be discussed by the tax authorities as companies may freely determine the prices to be applied
May be adjusted by the tax authorities up to 17.5%, this being the average between 10% and 25%
explanation: Comparability is the key condition. If the transactions are sufficiently comparable (same functions, risks, products, contractual conditions), the lower margin may be challenged. If not comparable enough, no adjustment is warranted. There is no automatic adjustment to the midpoint — the result should fall within the arm's length range.

question: The Inclusive Framework:
options: Is the new standard transfer pricing documentation in accordance with BEPS recommendations
Is a program developed by the European Union in order to smooth the incorporation of new countries
Is a cooperative project aimed at ensuring an effective implementation of the BEPS measures through the participation of all interested countries including developing economies
Refers to the analysis to be carried out in order to determine if transactions carried out between related parties have been agreed upon at arm's length
explanation: The OECD/G20 Inclusive Framework on BEPS is a multilateral body allowing all interested countries (including developing economies) to participate on an equal footing in implementing the BEPS measures and addressing remaining tax challenges including the two-pillar solution.

question: The two-pillar solution:
options: Is aimed at ensuring a fairer distribution of profits and taxing rights among countries aligning taxation with real business activity and value creation as well as a minimum global taxation
Is the new standard for Transfer Pricing documentation provided by the Inclusive Framework and to be generally implemented through the Multilateral Instrument
Provides for new rules in order to optimize the functional analysis to be carried out by multinational groups in order to set up their transfer pricing policies
Refers to the EU VAT action plan which pursues at the same time tackling fraud as well as introducing simplification measures
explanation: The two-pillar solution: Pillar 1 reallocates taxing rights over profits of large MNEs to market jurisdictions (fairer distribution); Pillar 2 introduces a 15% global minimum effective tax rate for MNE groups with revenue >€750M (minimum global taxation). Together they address both allocation and tax competition.

question: When a Double Tax Convention applies:
options: Conflicts of residency will be solved under Article 4 based upon nationality and visa permits
Tax residency must be determined in accordance with domestic rules even if those lead to double residency situations which will be solved under the Convention rules
The definition of tax residency provided by Article 4 will apply disregarding domestic law provisions
Only conflicts of residency affecting individuals can be solved as according to the 2017 OECD Model there is no possibility to solve a conflict of residency affecting legal entities
explanation: Article 4 OECD Model: each country first applies its own domestic rules to determine residency. If both countries claim the person, the tie-breaker rules in the DTC resolve the conflict. Domestic law comes first — the DTC resolves conflicts but does not replace domestic rules. Note: the 2017 OECD Model changed the tie-breaker for companies to a mutual agreement procedure.

question: A Portuguese company receives a dividend from its Belgian subsidiary. The DTC follows OECD Model and EU Parent-Subsidiary Directive conditions are met:
options: According to the EU Parent Subsidiary Directive's provisions the elimination of both legal and economic double taxation is ensured
Dividends will only be taxable in Portugal so that legal and economic double taxation is avoided
Dividends will be taxable in both countries but the Portuguese company will be able to deduct the tax paid in Belgium
Dividends will be taxable in Belgium but not in Portugal according to the EU Parent Subsidiary Directive thus allowing to avoid both legal and economic double taxation
explanation: The Parent-Subsidiary Directive: (1) eliminates withholding tax in Belgium (source) on dividend distribution — so no SO in Belgium, and (2) requires Portugal to either exempt the dividend or provide a credit for the underlying tax — so no PO double taxation. Result: dividends may be taxable in Belgium at corporate level (underlying tax) but not as a distribution, and not in Portugal — eliminating both legal and economic double taxation.

question: We can say that the underlying tax:
options: Refers to the withholding tax paid at the source country on a dividend distribution
May lead to economic double taxation unless it is neutralized at the parent company's residence country level when dividends are received from subsidiaries
Is the Corporate Income Tax paid by a company when the lowest marginal rate is applicable
Is the tax paid by a company in a low tax jurisdiction
explanation: The underlying tax is the corporate income tax paid by the subsidiary on its profits before distribution. When those profits are distributed as dividends and taxed again at the parent level, economic double taxation arises. The Parent-Subsidiary Directive and participation exemptions neutralize this effect.

question: An individual earns rental income of €1,000 in country A (taxed at 30% there). At residence country B, income of €1,000 with 25% rate. Country B applies the limited tax credit method:
options: Will get at the residence country a full deduction of the taxes paid abroad
The individual will not be allowed to any tax deduction at the residence country given that the tax rate applicable at the source country is higher than at the residence country
Total income obtained will be taxable at the residence country and double taxation will not be fully avoided
At the residence country will only declare income therein obtained and will get a deduction of 5% of the taxes paid abroad (difference between the applicable tax rates)
explanation: Limited tax credit: PO on foreign income = €1,000 × 25% = €250. Foreign tax paid = €300. Credit is limited to €250 (the lower amount). SO already paid = €300. Net PO = €250 - €250 = €0. But €300 was paid at source vs €250 that would have been due locally — the extra €50 is unrelieved. Total tax = €300 + €250 (on local income) = €550 vs €500 under full exemption. Double taxation is not fully eliminated.

question: In general terms, we can say that VAT:
options: Is a direct tax and is levied considering both the Residence and the Source Obligation principle
Covers national and international transactions as well as custom duties
Is applied all over Europe but each country has full freedom to set up its own rules thus harmonization not being possible at the level of the European Union
Is a tax borne by the final consumer and, even though it does not represent a charge for the business, it is fractionally collected in each business transaction
explanation: VAT is an indirect tax (not direct), borne by the final consumer, collected fractionally at each stage of the supply chain. Businesses charge and deduct VAT — so it is not a net cost for them. VAT is harmonized by EU Directives (not fully free for each country). Custom duties are separate from VAT.

question: The Multilateral Instrument:
options: Is the new model to be taken into account by countries when negotiating bilateral tax conventions
Is an agreement entered into by the taxpayer with the tax authorities of several countries providing prior clearance about the transfer pricing policy
Is the instrument providing for the updating of the existing bilateral double tax conventions to incorporate BEPS recommendations and affects any and all tax conventions on a worldwide basis

Is the instrument providing for the updating of the existing bilateral double tax conventions in order to incorporate BEPS recommendations for those countries being a party and with regard to the tax conventions expressly identified
explanation: The MLI (BEPS Action 15) modifies existing bilateral DTCs to incorporate BEPS recommendations — but only for countries that have signed the MLI AND only for the specific treaties they have identified as Covered Tax Agreements. It does not apply universally to all tax conventions worldwide. Option b describes an APA.

question: The OECD Guidelines recommend the application of the arm's length principle to transactions carried out by multinational groups:
options: Only when those transactions involve the use of low tax jurisdictions
Only when there are differences in the tax rates applying in the different countries where the Group's companies are based
In any case and with reference to any kind of transaction entered into within the Group except for those relating to intangibles as per the BEPS recommendations on Action 8
In any case and with reference to any kind of transaction entered into within the Group such as goods or services supplies, financial or intangible transactions
explanation: The arm's length principle applies to ALL intragroup transactions — goods, services, intangibles, and financial transactions — regardless of whether low-tax jurisdictions are involved or whether there are tax rate differences. BEPS Actions 8-10 actually strengthened the arm's length principle for intangibles, not excluded them.
